examples.title: Basic su command usage
examples.description: Demonstrates basic switching to root user
examples.code:
# Switch to root user (keeps current environment)
su root

# Switch to root with full login environment (RECOMMENDED)
su -
su - root
examples.explanation: First form preserves current directory and environment variables. Second form (with -) loads root's complete environment including PATH and HOME directory.

examples.title: Execute single command with su -c
examples.description: Running a specific command as another user without interactive shell
examples.code:
# List root directory as root user
su -c 'ls -la /root' root

# Create a file as another user
su -c 'touch /home/john/test.txt' john

# Combined with other options
su - -c 'systemctl restart apache2' root
examples.explanation: The -c option executes only the specified command and returns to the original shell. Useful for one-off administrative tasks without starting a full interactive session.

examples.title: su with environment preservation
examples.description: Using -m or -p option to preserve current environment
examples.code:
# Preserve current environment when switching
su -m root
su -p root

# Check current environment after switch
env | grep -E 'PATH|HOME|USER'
examples.explanation: The -m (or -p) option preserves the current user's environment variables when switching, rather than loading the target user's environment.

examples.title: Switch to specific non-root user
examples.description: How to switch to any non-root user account
examples.code:
# Switch to regular user 'john'
su - john

# Switch to user without login shell
su john

# Execute command as specific user
su -c 'whoami' john
su -c 'id' john
examples.explanation: Switching to non-root users works the same way. Requires knowing the target user's password unless running as root.

examples.title: Verify current user identity
examples.description: Commands to check current user after su
examples.code:
# Show current username
whoami

# Show detailed user and group IDs
id

# Show last few login entries
last | head

# Check process info
ps aux | grep su
examples.explanation: After using su, verify identity with these commands. 'whoami' shows effective user, 'id' shows UID, GID, and group memberships.
